_doc: Composition skill quality-gate scenarios (extends context_budget_scenarios.json's shape) — each scenario probes whether the agent follows a specific rule stated in services/chat-service/app/services/composition_skill.py, not general chat quality.

scenarios.ground_truth: Tests the 'Prose: TWO different tools, do not conflate them' rule. Turn 1 asks for the platform's grounded engine to draft the scene — the agent should call `composition_generate` (passing exactly one of `outline_node_id` or `chapter_id`), which is a confirm-gated, LLM-spend, drafter+critic pass grounded in the book's actual lore/canon/outline — NOT `composition_write_prose`. If the target chapter has no outline yet, the agent should say it needs to build a chapter node plus at least one scene node first (via `composition_outline_node_create`) before `composition_generate` can ground against it, rather than calling generate blind or refusing outright. Turn 2 asks the agent to author a short alternate line itself right now — this is text the MODEL composes, so it must NOT call `composition_generate` again; the correct path is to write the text in its own reply and, if saving it, call `composition_write_prose` with `expected_draft_version` obtained from a prior `composition_get_prose` read — never blind-overwrite, and never call `write_prose` to simulate what `generate` would have produced.
turns: Can you run the platform's grounded generation engine to draft the opening scene of chapter 3? I want it to actually use the book's canon and outline, not something you make up yourself.
Nice. Now, separately, just write me one alternate opening line yourself, right now in your reply — no need to run the engine for this one, I just want a quick draft from you.

scenarios.ground_truth: Tests the 'project_id, not book_id — the most common mistake' rule. Most composition tools (e.g. `composition_list_outline`) take `project_id` (the Work's primary key), not `book_id`. The user's request only supplies book-level framing. The agent must first resolve the Work via `composition_get_work(book_id=...)` (which returns, or idempotently creates via `composition_create_work`, the Work and its `project_id`) and THEN call `composition_list_outline(project_id=..., detail="summary")` using the resolved project_id. It must not: (a) error out saying it lacks a project_id, (b) invent/guess a project_id, or (c) pass the book_id directly where project_id is required.
turns: Show me the outline structure for this book — arcs, chapters, scenes, the whole tree.

scenarios.ground_truth: Tests that the agent surfaces the correct reversibility caveat per resource type before acting, per the 'Canon rules' and 'Motif library' sections. Turn 1 asks to delete a canon rule: `composition_canon_rule_delete` (soft-archive) has NO restore tool for canon rules, so the agent must explicitly warn the user this is not reversible (no restore tool exists) and confirm before calling delete — it must not claim it can be undone later. Turn 2 asks to archive a motif: `composition_motif_archive` IS de-facto reversible — there's no dedicated 'restore' tool, but `composition_motif_patch(motif_id, expected_version, status="active")` un-archives it — so the agent should say the motif can be un-archived later (mentioning the patch-based un-archive) rather than treating it with the same irreversibility warning it gave the canon rule, and should not claim a dedicated restore tool exists.
turns: Delete the canon rule about the empire's succession law — we don't need it anymore.
Also archive the 'chosen one refuses the call' motif from this book, it's not being used.

scenarios.ground_truth: Tests the 'Outline structure' rule that `composition_outline_node_update` REQUIRES `expected_version` and that a stale version is rejected (`outcome: "applied_conflict"`), not silently overwritten — i.e. 'read before you write'. The user gives a new synopsis but no version number. The agent must NOT call `composition_outline_node_update` with a guessed, omitted, or hallucinated `expected_version`. Correct behavior is to first read the node's current state (e.g. `composition_get_outline_node`, which returns the node's `version`) and only then call `composition_outline_node_update` passing that just-read `expected_version` along with the new synopsis.
turns: Update the synopsis of the chapter 3 outline node to: 'The heist goes wrong when the informant betrays the crew.'

scenarios.ground_truth: Tests the 'What you genuinely cannot do here (say so, don't guess a tool name)' rule. Style profile, character voice profile, reference-source pins, scene grounding pins, and per-entity generation overrides have NO MCP tool — they are Studio-UI/REST-only. The user asks to set a style profile ('make it sound more like grimdark noir') for future generation. The agent must NOT invent or guess a tool call (e.g. no fake 'set_style_profile' or similar) and must NOT silently ignore the request or pretend it did something. Correct behavior is to tell the user plainly that this specific control lives in the Studio UI, not in a tool available to it in chat, and optionally point them there.
turns: Can you set the style profile for this book's generation to sound more like grimdark noir from now on?

scenarios.ground_truth: Tests the 'Motifs are NOT connected to PlanForge' rule. The user asks to make a motif recur across every arc of their PlanForge plan. The agent must explain that motifs are not connected to PlanForge — a plan cannot declare that a motif recurs in an arc — and that if the user wants a motif woven in, the correct path is to bind it with `composition_motif_bind(project_id, node_id, motif_id, role_bindings)` per-chapter AFTER the plan is compiled, not as part of the planning flow itself. The agent must not attempt to call a PlanForge (`plan_*`) tool to attach a motif to the plan, and must not fabricate a tool that links motifs into PlanForge.
turns: I want the 'reluctant mentor' motif to recur throughout every arc in my PlanForge plan — can you add that to the plan so it's baked in?
